summary:
The Roman Siege of Syracuse (214–212 BC) was one of the most technically remarkable sieges of antiquity, famous for the defensive innovations of [[event:3799|Archimedes Inventions]]'s Archimedes, which held off the Roman fleet and army for two years. Roman general Marcus Claudius Marcellus attacked by land and sea with a large force, expecting a quick conquest of the wealthy Sicilian city, but Archimedes had designed a series of war machines that confounded every Roman approach: catapults of varied range and power rained down missiles, iron claws attached to long chains grabbed Roman ships and overturned them, and 'burning mirrors' (possibly large polished shields concentrating sunlight) allegedly set ships afire.

Marcellus reportedly said in frustration that Archimedes was 'a geometrical Briareus' — a hundred-handed giant using mathematics as his weapon. The city eventually fell when Roman troops found an unguarded section of wall during a festival. Archimedes was killed by a soldier despite Marcellus's explicit orders to spare him — tradition holds he was killed while drawing geometric diagrams in the sand and refusing to be disturbed ('Do not disturb my circles'). Marcellus was reportedly moved to tears by his death and reportedly honored him with a tomb. The fall of Syracuse brought Sicily firmly under Roman control and its artistic treasures to Rome.
